title: Potato scones
subtitle: Scottish pan-fried flatbreads made from mashed potato and a little plain flour
excerpt: Scottish potato scones (tattie scones) are made from freshly mashed potato mixed with a small amount of flour and a little butter while still warm. The dough is rolled thin, cut into triangles or rounds, and cooked in a dry pan. The result is a soft, pliable flatbread with a mild potato flavour and a slightly floury surface. Served warm as part of a cooked Scottish breakfast or buttered as a side. The dough must be worked while the potato is still warm.
sourceNotes: Potato scones (tattie scones) are a traditional Scottish griddle preparation. F Marian McNeill, The Scots Kitchen (1929), describes them as a staple of the Scottish cooked breakfast and tea table, made from leftover boiled potatoes. The combination of mashed potato, flour, and butter cooked on a dry girdle (griddle) is consistent across Scottish household cookery literature of the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cooled potato scones for up to 1 month. Toast from frozen or fry in a little butter from frozen.
recipe.batchNotes: Make a large batch using leftover boiled potatoes. Cook and freeze.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten the day they are made. Reheat in a dry pan or under the grill.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: cooked in a dry (ungreased) frying pan or griddle over medium heat; no oven required. Cook for about 3 to 4 minutes per side until pale golden with dark spots.

glossaryTerms.definition: A well-made potato scone should be pliable and soft when warm, bending without cracking. If the scone breaks when you try to fold it, there is too much flour or the potato was not warm enough when the dough was made. The dough must be mixed while the potato is still hot because warm potato absorbs less flour: cold mashed potato is drier and requires more flour to hold together, resulting in a stiffer, bread-like scone rather than a thin, tender one. Work quickly and mix only until the dough comes together. Roll it as thin as you dare: 4 to 5mm is the target.

content.text: Work while the potato is hot:

and produces a softer, more tender scone. Cold mashed potato makes a stiffer dough that is harder to roll thin. These are best eaten immediately but also excellent cold and fried in butter the next day.

attrs.prepNote: boiled and mashed while still hot, no milk or butter added yet

content.text: While the mashed potato is still warm, mix in the melted butter and salt. Add the flour and mix quickly until a soft dough forms. Turn out onto a lightly floured surface. Roll or pat the dough to about 4 to 5mm thick. Cut into rounds with a cutter or into triangles by cutting the dough into a large circle and then into wedges.

Heat a heavy frying pan or griddle over medium heat; do not grease it. Cook the potato scones in batches for 3 to 4 minutes per side until pale golden with brown spots. They should feel dry and firm on the outside but soft in the centre. Transfer to a rack or tea towel. Serve warm with butter, or as part of a cooked breakfast alongside eggs and bacon.
